summary: Gruber defends Apple's iPhone 4S against criticism that it should have been an 'iPhone 5' with a larger screen, LTE, or new form factor. He argues Apple's decisions on screen size and battery life reflect deliberate engineering trade-offs, not falling behind Android. The piece examines Apple's two-year product cycle, Tim Cook's growth-oriented leadership, the post-Jobs executive team finding its roles, Siri as a strategic move against Google search, and the 4S camera as the most compelling upgrade over the iPhone 4. Written largely before Steve Jobs's death, it captures a pivotal moment of transition at Apple.
key_points: Apple's 3.5-inch screen is a deliberate design choice optimizing one-handed use, not a failure to keep up with Android's larger displays
The iPhone now follows a two-year cycle: new form factor one year, improved internals the next, targeting the upgrade audience on expiring contracts rather than annual upgraders
Tim Cook is playing offense with enormous growth opportunities, not defense of a precarious peak
Siri represents Apple's first real move against Google's search dominance by routing queries through Yelp and Wolfram Alpha instead of Google
The 4S camera is positioned to genuinely compete with dedicated point-and-shoot cameras, and is likely the most meaningful upgrade over the iPhone 4
money_quotes: People who claim to be disappointed that Apple's 2011 new iPhone doesn't have a bigger display or LTE are effectively arguing that the iPhone should be more like Android.

Apple pursues timeless style, not fleeting trendiness. This iPhone design might be like that of the Porsche 911 — a distinctive, iconic, timeless, instantly-recognizable representation of the product's brand itself.
Normal people don't buy brand-new $700 smartphones each and every year.
Apple's brand of luxury is mass market luxury. Apple's stores are crowded. They're bustling. They're loud. And they're inclusive, not exclusive.
Every Siri query that's answered by Yelp or Wolfram Alpha is a query that might otherwise have been answered by Google.
Siri isn't so much competing with Google search as trying to stake out new territory beyond Google search.
Steve Jobs's charisma and personal brand are so enormous that they rival those of the company itself.
Cook replaced Jobs as chief executive, but it's Schiller who had to replace him as chief product pitchman.
key_insight: The iPhone 4S represents Apple's deliberate refusal to chase spec-sheet competition with Android, instead optimizing for real-world trade-offs in a two-year product cycle aimed at the mass market rather than annual upgraders.
